title: Wii Backup Loaders
requirements: A Wii with the Homebrew Channel installed
cIOS installed (d2x cIOS v10 beta 52 or later recommended)
An SD card (any size up to 32GB)
A USB storage device (HDD or flash drive — 2.5" drives without external power need a Y-cable)
A Wii Remote and sensor bar
Game backups (dumped from your own discs)
warnings: !!Do NOT!! share game backups — only use dumps of games you own.
USB flash drives can be unreliable with USB Loader GX. A proper USB HDD is recommended.
FAT32 is the most compatible format for the USB drive. NTFS works but some features may be limited.
_note: Wii Backup Loaders let you play game backups from a USB drive or SD card. USB Loader GX is the most popular; WiiFlow Lite has a flashy coverflow UI. Both need cIOS installed first.

steps.title: Before you start
steps.body:
**Wii Backup Loaders** let you launch game backups from a USB drive or SD card. You can load games faster, apply cheats, use custom textures, force video modes, and more.

This guide covers **USB Loader GX** and **WiiFlow Lite** — the two main backup loaders for the Wii. Both need **cIOS** (custom IOS) installed to work. If you haven't installed cIOS yet, follow a cIOS guide first and come back here.

Graphite handles downloading the loader files and setting up your SD card. You just do the on-console steps.

steps.title: Put the loader files on your SD card
steps.body:
Graphite downloads **USB Loader GX** and/or **WiiFlow Lite** and unpacks them into the right `apps` folders on your SD card.

You only need one of them, but both fit easily. Press the button and wait.

steps.title: Clean up and eject the card
steps.body:
macOS scatters hidden junk files (`.DS_Store`, `._` files) onto SD cards, and Wii homebrew can choke on them.

Press the button, and Graphite strips those files off the card and **safely ejects it for you**. Once it pops off, pull the card out.

steps.title: Move the SD card to your Wii
steps.body:
Take the SD card out of your computer and slot it into the **SD card slot on the front of the Wii**. A USB adapter won't work — it must be the built-in slot.

Plug your USB storage device into one of the USB ports on the back (bottom USB port is recommended).

Press **I did this** once both are connected.

steps.title: Prepare your game files
steps.body:
Game backups need to be in a specific folder structure on your USB drive or SD card:

```
wbfs/GameName [GameID]/gameid.wbfs
```

For example:
```
wbfs/The Legend of Zelda Twilight Princess [GZ2P01]/GZ2P01.wbfs
```

The folder name doesn't matter beyond the `[GameID]` part, but the file inside **must** be named `GameID.wbfs` (or `GameID.wbf1` for split files). The `wbfs` folder goes in the **root** of your USB drive or SD card.

You can split larger games with **Wii Backup Manager** (Windows) or **Wit** (cross-platform). USB Loader GX and WiiFlow both support split WBFS files.

steps.title: Launch USB Loader GX
steps.body:
Power on your Wii and open the **Homebrew Channel**.

Select **USB Loader GX** from the app grid. It loads and scans your USB drive for games.

If no games appear, make sure the USB drive has a `wbfs` folder with game files in it. If the loader hangs, your USB drive might not be compatible — try reformatting to FAT32 or use a different drive.

steps.title: Configure USB Loader GX
steps.body:
USB Loader GX works out of the box, but you may want to adjust a few settings:

1. Press **Settings** (the gear icon) on the main screen.
2. Under **Loader Settings**:
   - **Game Load** → set to your cIOS version (e.g. 249 or 250)
   - **Game Partition** → set to your USB drive
3. Under **Video**:
   - **Video Mode** → set to **Force NTSC** or **Force PAL** depending on your region
   - **TV Mode** → choose **480p** if using component cables
4. Under **Custom Paths**:
   - Make sure **Game Path** points to `usb1:/wbfs` (or `sd:/wbfs` if using SD)

Press **B** or **Save** to go back. Select a game and press **Start** to play.

steps.title: Launch WiiFlow Lite
steps.body:
From the **Homebrew Channel**, select **WiiFlow Lite**.

WiiFlow scans your USB drive and displays your games in a coverflow view. Scroll through your collection with the D-pad.

If no games appear, check that your `wbfs` folder is in the right place. WiiFlow also supports gamecube games via Nintendont — place them in `games/` on the root of your USB drive.

steps.title: Configure WiiFlow Lite
steps.body:
WiiFlow also works out of the box, but here are useful settings:

1. Press **Home** on the Wii Remote, then select **Settings**.
2. Under **Main Settings**:
   - **Custom IOS** → set to your cIOS (e.g. 249)
3. Under **Game Settings**:
   - **GameCube Mode** → set to **Nintendont** if you want to play GC games
   - **USB Device** → make sure it points to your USB drive
4. Under **Startup Settings**:
   - Enable **Auto Load** to boot straight to the coverflow

Press **Save** and exit. Select a game to launch.

steps.title: You're done
steps.body:
Your Wii is set up to play game backups from a USB drive. You can use **USB Loader GX**, **WiiFlow Lite**, or both — they share the same `wbfs` folder.

If games fail to load:
- Make sure **cIOS** is installed correctly (run the cIOS installer again and verify the version)
- Try a different **USB port** (bottom port is more compatible)
- Reformat your USB drive to **FAT32** with 32KB cluster size
- Some USB flash drives simply don't work — switch to a proper HDD
- Ensure your game dumps are valid and in the correct `wbfs/GameName [GameID]/gameid.wbfs` structure

For Wii games on a **Wii U's vWii**, the process is the same — just launch the loaders from the vWii Homebrew Channel.
